claims.normalized_claim: Michael Milken built and championed the high-yield ('junk') bond market: his insight was that a diversified basket of low-rated, below-investment-grade bonds paid investors enough extra interest to more than offset the ones that defaulted, so he financed companies the big banks had shut out by helping them issue high-yield debt.
counterevidence: The long-run net return of high-yield portfolios is debated and varies by period; frame Milken's claim as his thesis, supported by the research he cited.
allowed_wording: He built the high-yield, or 'junk', bond market.
A basket of these low-rated bonds paid enough to more than cover the defaults.
prohibited_wording: Junk bonds are guaranteed to outperform.
claims.notes: Core idea. 'Junk' is the market's own term; keep neutral.

claims.normalized_claim: Milken ran Drexel Burnham Lambert's high-yield operation from Beverly Hills at a now-famous X-shaped trading desk; the high-yield market grew over the 1980s into one worth well over a hundred billion dollars, and his 1987 compensation was reported at around $550 million, described at the time as the largest single-year pay on record in America.
evidence_locations: Bruck (Beverly Hills, X-shaped desk, market growth)
counterevidence: The exact market-size figure varies by source and year; use 'well over a hundred billion' as a conservative characterization.
allowed_wording: His 1987 pay was reported at around $550 million.
He ran the operation from Beverly Hills, at an X-shaped desk.
prohibited_wording: He was the richest man in the world.
claims.notes: $550M is a single-year (1987) compensation figure, contemporaneously reported as a record.

claims.normalized_claim: Drexel pioneered the 'highly confident letter' — a letter stating the firm was highly confident it could raise the financing for a given takeover — and Milken's high-yield debt fueled the 1980s wave of corporate takeovers and leveraged buyouts, letting smaller players bid for much larger companies.
counterevidence: Whether the takeover wave was net good or harmful is contested — attribute both views (see CLM-0013).
allowed_wording: Drexel perfected the 'highly confident' letter.
His money fueled the 1980s takeover era.
prohibited_wording: The 'highly confident letter' was a legal guarantee.
Every takeover he financed destroyed jobs.
claims.notes: 'highly confident' is a direct phrase; quote in quotes, attribute to Drexel.

claims.normalized_claim: Ivan Boesky, a prominent Wall Street arbitrageur, pleaded guilty in 1986 in his own securities-fraud case and cooperated with the government, and his cooperation pointed investigators toward Michael Milken.
allowed_wording: Ivan Boesky pleaded guilty in 1986 and cooperated, pointing investigators toward Milken.
Boesky started naming others.
claims.notes: Boesky's plea/cooperation is fact; what it 'pointed toward' is allegation until adjudicated — keep as investigative lead.

claims.normalized_claim: In 1989 a federal grand jury charged Milken in a 98-count indictment that included RICO racketeering as well as securities fraud, mail fraud, insider trading, stock parking, and tax counts; these were charges (accusations), not findings, and the case never went to trial.
allowed_wording: He was charged in a 98-count indictment that included RICO.
Those 98 counts were accusations, not findings; the case never went to trial.
claims.notes: WORDING LOCK: CHARGED (98), not convicted. RICO was a CHARGE only.

claims.normalized_claim: In April 1990 Milken pleaded guilty to six felony counts (securities and reporting violations, conspiracy, mail fraud, and a tax count); the RICO charge and the other 92 counts were dropped. He did NOT plead guilty to a standalone insider-trading count and did NOT plead guilty to racketeering/RICO.
evidence_locations: NYT 'Milken Pleads Guilty to 6 Felony Counts'
allowed_wording: He pleaded guilty to six felony counts.
The RICO charge and the other 92 counts were dropped.
prohibited_wording: He was convicted of insider trading.
claims.notes: WORDING LOCK: PLEADED to SIX. No standalone insider-trading or RICO conviction. Distinguish from CLM-0005.

claims.normalized_claim: Milken's penalty totaled about $600 million — a $200 million fine plus a $400 million fund to compensate those harmed — and the SEC permanently barred him for life from the securities industry. (Later, separate civil settlements are not part of this ~$600M figure.)
counterevidence: Milken later paid additional sums in separate civil matters; keep the ~$600M figure scoped to the 1990 criminal resolution.
allowed_wording: The penalty came to about $600 million: a $200M fine and a $400M restitution fund.
He was barred for life from the securities industry.
prohibited_wording: He paid $600 million in fines (the restitution fund is not a fine).
claims.notes: ~$600M = $200M fine + $400M restitution. SEC lifetime ban is permanent and was NOT lifted by the 2020 pardon.

claims.normalized_claim: Milken was sentenced to 10 years in prison; the sentence was later reduced after he cooperated with prosecutors and for good behavior, and he ultimately served about two years.
allowed_wording: He was sentenced to ten years.
The sentence was reduced and he served about two years.
claims.notes: 10-year sentence, reduced; ~2 years served at a minimum-security facility.

claims.normalized_claim: On February 18, 2020, President Donald Trump granted Michael Milken a full presidential pardon. A pardon is an act of clemency, not a declaration of innocence or exoneration: it forgives the punishment but does not erase the guilty plea, and this pardon did not lift the SEC's lifetime industry ban.
allowed_wording: In February 2020 he received a full presidential pardon.
A pardon forgives the crime; it does not erase the guilty plea, and it did not lift the SEC lifetime ban.
prohibited_wording: The pardon proved he was innocent.
The pardon ended his SEC ban.
claims.notes: WORDING LOCK: clemency, not innocence; plea stands; SEC ban unaffected.

claims.normalized_claim: The White House pardon statement praised Milken's philanthropy, described his offenses as 'novel' technical and regulatory violations, and listed numerous prominent supporters who had advocated for clemency. (The statement's 'pleaded guilty in 1989' is imprecise; the plea was April 1990.)
counterevidence: The 'novel offenses' framing is the White House's advocacy characterization, contested by critics; attribute it, do not adopt it.
allowed_wording: The White House praised his philanthropy and called the offenses novel.
It listed powerful supporters who had pushed for the pardon.
prohibited_wording: His offenses were merely technical (stated as fact).
Everyone agreed he deserved the pardon.
claims.notes: Attribute ALL framing to the White House statement; flag the 1989/1990 imprecision; never narrate 'novel/technical' as neutral fact.

claims.normalized_claim: Soon after his release, Milken was diagnosed with advanced prostate cancer with a prognosis reportedly measured in months; he founded what became the Prostate Cancer Foundation, which grew into the largest private (philanthropic) funder of prostate cancer research.
counterevidence: The exact prognosis is reported (often 'a year to 18 months'); use 'reportedly measured in months'.
allowed_wording: He was diagnosed with advanced prostate cancer; the prognosis was reportedly months.
He founded what became the Prostate Cancer Foundation.
claims.notes: 'reportedly' for the prognosis; foundation = leading private prostate-cancer-research funder.

claims.normalized_claim: Milken had earlier created the Milken Institute, an economic think tank that carries his name; he is a major economic and medical philanthropist and, decades after a prognosis of months, remains alive.
allowed_wording: He created the Milken Institute, an economic think tank.
Decades later, he is still alive.
prohibited_wording: He runs the financial industry again.
He was cured by his own foundation.
claims.notes: Living-person, present tense; verify 'still alive' at publish (R3 recheck).

claims.normalized_claim: Whether Michael Milken is best understood as a misunderstood financial genius scapegoated by the system, or as the face of 1980s greed who was forgiven because of wealth and influence, is a contested, attributed debate — not a settled fact. Narration must attribute it ('defenders argue / critics argue') and never resolve it as truth.
counterevidence: By design this claim has no single answer; that is the episode's thesis.
allowed_wording: Defenders argue he was a scapegoated genius; critics argue he was the face of greed.
Both views describe the same facts.
prohibited_wording: He was definitely a genius/criminal (as settled fact).
History has decided he was innocent/guilty.
claims.notes: WORDING LOCK: always attributed, never resolved in narration. The ending hands the verdict to the viewer.

findings: R3 wording locks applied: 'pleaded guilty' (never 'convicted at trial'); pleaded to SIX felonies (never 'convicted of insider trading' or 'convicted of RICO'); CHARGED (98, incl. RICO) vs PLEADED (6) kept distinct; RICO + 92 counts dropped.
Penalty ~$600M = $200M fine + $400M restitution; SEC LIFETIME BAN remains and was NOT lifted by the pardon. Sentenced 10y, reduced, ~2y served.
2020 pardon = clemency, NOT innocence/exoneration; guilty plea STANDS. White House 'novel/technical' framing and 'pleaded guilty in 1989' are attributed/flagged (actual plea April 1990).
Genius-vs-greed is ATTRIBUTED throughout (CLM-0013), never narrated as settled fact; ending hands the verdict to the viewer.
LIVING person (R3): before publish, re-confirm 'still alive' status and lock verbatim White House/court/SEC quotes; durable source content hashes PENDING (capture before broadcast).
